content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1вҖ“3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/2111.11004
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/2111.11004/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1вҖ“3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDFвҶ’md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it вҖ” pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone вҖ” proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1вҖ“3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1вҖ“2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3вҖ“5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/2111.11004

Begin by reading paper.md.

    25	## Abstract
    26	
    27	Gradient temporal difference (Gradient TD) algorithms are a popular class of stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms used for policy evaluation in reinforcement learning. Here, we consider Gradient TD algorithms with an additional heavy ball momentum term and provide choice of step size and momentum parameter that ensures almost sure convergence of these algorithms asymptotically. In doing so, we decompose the heavy ball Gradient TD iterates into three separate iterates with different step sizes. We first analyze these iterates under one-timescale SA setting using results from current literature. However, the one-timescale case is restrictive and a more general analysis can be provided by looking at a three-timescale decomposition of the iterates. In the process we provide the first conditions for stability and convergence of general threetimescale SA. We then prove that the heavy ball Gradient TD algorithm is convergent using our three-timescale SA analysis. Finally, we evaluate these algorithms on standard RL problems and report improvement in performance over the vanilla algorithms.
    28	
    29	## 1 Introduction
    30	
    31	In reinforcement learning (RL), the goal of the learner or the agent is to maximize its long term accumulated reward by interacting with the environment. One important task in most of RL algorithms is that of policy evaluation . It predicts the average accumulated reward an agent would receive from a state (called value function ) if it follows the given policy. In model-free learning , the agent does not have access to the underlying dynamics of the environment and has to learn the value function from samples of the form (state, action, reward, next-state). Two very popular algorithms in the model-free setting are Monte-Carlo (MC) and temporal difference (TD) learning (see Sutton and Barto (2018), Sutton (1988)). It is a well known fact that TD learning diverges in the offpolicy setting (see Baird (1995)). A class of algorithms called gradient temporal difference (Gradient TD) were introduced in (Sutton, Maei, and SzepesvВҙ ari 2009) and (Sutton et al. 2009) which are convergent even in the off-policy setting. These algorithms fall under a larger class of algorithms called linear stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms.

   319	## 6 Related Work and Conclusion
   320	
   321	To the best of our knowledge no previous work has specifically looked at Gradient TD methods with an added heavy ball term. The use of momentum specifically in the SA setting is very limited. Section 4.1 of (Mou et al. 2020) does talk about momentum; however the problem looked at is that of SGD with momentum and the driving matrix is assumed to be symmetric (see Appendix H of their paper). We do not make any such assumption here. The work of (Devraj, BuЛҮ sВҙ ДұВҙ c, and Meyn 2019), indeed looks at momentum in SA setting. However, they introduce a matrix momentum term which is not equivalent to heavy ball momentum. Acceleration in Gradient TD methods has been looked at in (Pan, White, and White 2017). The authors provide a new algorithm called ATD and the acceleration is in form of better data efficiency. However, they do not make use of momentum methods.
   322	
   323	In this work we have introduced heavy ball momentum in Gradient Temporal difference algorithms for the first time. We decompose the two iterates of these algorithms into three separate iterates and provide asymptotic convergence guarantees of these new schemes under the same assumptions made by their vanilla counterparts. Specifically, we show convergence in the One-TS regime as well as Three-TS regime. In both the cases, the momentum parameter gradually goes 1. Three-TS formulation gives us more flexibility in choosing the momentum parameter. Specifically, compared to the One-TS setting, a larger momentum parameter can be chosen during the initial phase in the Three-TS case. We observe improved performance with these new schemes when compared with the original algorithms.
   324	
   325	As a step forward from this work, the natural direction would be to look at more sophisticated momentum methods such as Nesterov's accelerated method (Nesterov 1983). Also, here we only provide the convergence guarantees of these new momentum methods. A particularly interesting step would be to quantify the benefits of using momentum in SA settings. Specifically, it would be interesting to extend weak convergence rate analysis of (Konda and Tsitsiklis 2004; Mokkadem and Pelletier 2006) to Three-TS regime. Also, extending the recent convergence rate results in expectation and high probability of GTD methods (Dalal et al. 2018b; Gupta, Srikant, and Ying 2019; Kaledin et al. 2019; Dalal, Szorenyi, and Thoppe 2020) to these momentum settings would be interesting works for the future.

27:Gradient temporal difference (Gradient TD) algorithms are a popular class of stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms used for policy evaluation in reinforcement learning. Here, we consider Gradient TD algorithms with an additional heavy ball momentum term and provide choice of step size and momentum parameter that ensures almost sure convergence of these algorithms asymptotically. In doing so, we decompose the heavy ball Gradient TD iterates into three separate iterates with different step sizes. We first analyze these iterates under one-timescale SA setting using results from current literature. However, the one-timescale case is restrictive and a more general analysis can be provided by looking at a three-timescale decomposition of the iterates. In the process we provide the first conditions for stability and convergence of general threetimescale SA. We then prove that the heavy ball Gradient TD algorithm is convergent using our three-timescale SA analysis. Finally, we evaluate these algorithms on standard RL problems and report improvement in performance over the vanilla algorithms.
41:The momentum parameter О· n was chosen to decompose the iterate into two recursions of the form given by (2) and (3). We use such a decomposition for Gradient TD methods with momentum. This leads to three separate iterates with three step-sizes. We analyze these three iterates and provide stability (iterates remain bounded throughout) and almost sure (a.s.) convergence guarantees.
47:- Finally, we evaluate these algorithms for different choice of step-size and momentum parameters on standard RL problems and report an improvement in performance over their vanilla counterparts.

317:In all the examples considered, the momentum methods outperform their vanilla counterparts. Since, in the Three-TS setting, a lower value of w can be chosen, this ensures that the momentum parameter is not small in the initial phase of the algorithm as in the One-TS setting. This in turn helps to reduce the RMSPBE faster in the initial phase of the algorithm as is evident from the experiments.
323:In this work we have introduced heavy ball momentum in Gradient Temporal difference algorithms for the first time. We decompose the two iterates of these algorithms into three separate iterates and provide asymptotic convergence guarantees of these new schemes under the same assumptions made by their vanilla counterparts. Specifically, we show convergence in the One-TS regime as well as Three-TS regime. In both the cases, the momentum parameter gradually goes 1. Three-TS formulation gives us more flexibility in choosing the momentum parameter. Specifically, compared to the One-TS setting, a larger momentum parameter can be chosen during the initial phase in the Three-TS case. We observe improved performance with these new schemes when compared with the original algorithms.
